text: Once upon a time, there was a man named Fan Liao, who was known for his utmost filial piety. His close relatives had passed away early, and he took care of his stepmother after his father remarried. The stepmother fell ill with a severe swelling that internally formed an abscess, causing unbearable pain and preventing her from sleeping day or night. Fan Liao became deeply distressed, and over the course of more than a month, his body became extremely thin and weak, to the point where people could hardly recognize him. He wanted to call a healer to perform acupuncture, but fearing the pain it might cause his stepmother, he instead suckled on the swelling on her body with his mouth, and she immediately felt slightly better. The pus continuously flowed out of the wound, and his stepmother was finally able to sleep peacefully at night. One night, she had a dream in which a ghostly child spoke to her: "If the wound is fed by a carp, you will recover and your life will be extended. If no carp is eaten, then death will ensue." Upon hearing this, Fan Liao was filled with worry and fear. He looked up to heaven and sighed, "My lack of filial piety has led to this. In the depths of winter, when ice is frozen, how can I get this fish to eat?" He embraced his stepmother's head and parted from her, crying as he went in and out, shedding tears of sorrow. He looked up to heaven and sighed again, "If heaven pities me, may the fish be moved to appear. I beg of the spirits to help." Fan Liao then removed his clothes and lay on the ice, but no fish appeared. He lay naked on the ice, determined to fulfill his plea. The heavens, moved by his filial piety, caused two carps to appear beneath his back. Joy arose in his heart, and he brought the carps back to feed his stepmother. He fed them to the wound, and she immediately recovered completely. Her life became long, and she lived a long time, eventually reaching the age of 110 before passing away. Fan Liao's filial piety was as unchanging as the pine and cypress trees, a testament to his enduring virtue.

There was once a man named Zhang Song from Longxi, who was known for his utmost filial piety. At the age of eight, his mother fell ill and lay in bed, suddenly craving purslane to eat. Upon hearing this, Zhang Song hurriedly ran to the ground to look for purslane, but found none. He then burst into tears and said, "Alas, my parents, who bore me and toiled for me! Now that she is ill, when will she recover? If heaven pities me, may the purslane grow." From dawn until noon, his cries did not cease. Heaven was moved by his filial piety and caused the purslane to grow out of season. He then took it back home to feed his mother with it. Because of eating the purslane, his mother recovered. Zhang Song grew up to be an adult, and his mother died of her illness. His family became wealthy. The coffins and tombs he made were all done by his own hands, without using the labor of servants.

A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. --- In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings. --- From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you. --- As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the *Shujing* (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the *Yijing* (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the *Liji* (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them. --- Zhang Song's burial process did not use carts, oxen, or human labor, but only the couple themselves, who carried their mother's coffin on their own strength and pushed it on a cart, with the wife pulling it towards the burial site. At the time, there was a sudden wind and heavy rain, and the mud was up to their knees, yet no dust rose on the path during the burial. After his mother's burial, Zhang Song carried soil to the tomb for three years, crying continuously and losing all his hair. The heavens recognized his filial piety and responded with thunder from the north of the tomb, and suddenly a cloud and wind arrived at his side, carrying him to a spot 80 steps east of the tomb. Then, with a thunderclap, the tomb opened, revealing the coffin, which had a message on its lid: "Zhang Song's filial piety is known to the divine, and his sincere filial piety has revived his mother, granting her an additional 32 years of life. She may now return to be cared for by her son." Everyone who heard about this event was moved to admiration, and since then, no such event has been heard of. The emperor appointed Zhang Song as the governor of Jin Cheng, and later promoted him to the position of Left Shangpu. The story is recorded in the *Zhi Zong Zhuan*.

Once upon a time, there was Jiao Hua, who was known for his extreme filial piety. He was from Chang'an and held the position of Left Shangshu during the late Eastern Han period. His father fell ill, and Jiao Hua, filled with filial devotion, cared for his parents tirelessly, never removing his clothes or hat, and was deeply distressed day and night, fearing the worst. When his father's condition worsened, Jiao Hua returned home and said, "Between the two of us, if my father does not recover, we will both die underground, who will then take care of him?" His father replied, "I am old and frail, and it would be too light a burden to leave you to bear alone, so do not speak of it. Recently, I have had bad dreams, and I know I will not survive. When I am still in good health, you should inform all our relatives, both inside and outside the family, and call those nearby to come and say our goodbyes."

Jiao Hua asked his father, "What bad dream did you have?" His father replied, "I dreamed that a divine being descended to take me, saying, 'If you want to survive, you must eat one meal of gourd, and then you will live. Without eating gourd, you won't survive for more than ten days.' It is now mid-winter, and how can I possibly get gourd to eat? Therefore, I know I am going to die." Hearing this, Jiao Hua choked with sorrow, unable to eat or drink, and his voice became hoarse and eventually ceased. For ten days, he remained unconscious. Later, he woke up and dreamed that a deity called out to him, "You have a filial heart, which has moved the heavens. The gods have sent me to bring you a pair of gourds. You should take them and give them to your father as medicine." In his dream, Jiao Hua knelt and bowed to receive the gourds. When he woke up, he found two gourds in his hands, filling the room with their fragrance. He presented them to his father, who ate the gourds and recovered from his illness. Hence, the saying goes, "In mid-winter, thinking of gourd, Jiao Hua, your father will eat it." All people must have a good heart, and filial piety is naturally rewarded by heaven. This story is recorded in the *Records of the Grand Historian*. In the time of Emperor Huang (Yellow Emperor), there was a skilled physician named Yu Fu, who could bring the dead back to life. After Yu Fu's death, there were other skilled physicians. By the time of the Warring States period, there was Bian Que. At the end of the Han dynasty, there was a physician who performed surgery, washing the five organs and cutting open the brain to remove worms, and was subsequently killed by Emperor Wei Wu (Cao Cao).

Once upon a time, there was Bian Que, an excellent and skilled physician who traveled throughout the country. Hearing that the crown prince of the state of Guo was ill and had already died for eight days, Bian Que then requested to be allowed to see him. Afterward, he came out and told people, "Although the crown prince is dead, he can still be revived." The ruler of Guo heard this and summoned Bian Que to revive the crown prince, who was indeed revived. The ruler of Guo was greatly pleased and immediately bestowed gold, silver, jewels, and jade upon Bian Que. Bian Que declined to accept them. The ruler of Guo said, "Now that I have revived my son, since nothing has gone wrong, why do you not accept them?" Bian Que replied, "The crown prince's life is not yet finished; it is not solely due to my efforts that he was revived." He left without accepting the gifts. Once upon a time, there was Guan Lu, whose courtesy name was Gongming, an excellent and talented person. At that time, in the middle of the sixth month, he went past a plain and saw a young man who was about eighteen or nineteen years old, cutting wheat on the south side of the road. Guan Lu sighed and passed by. The young man asked the old man, "Why did you sigh?" Guan Lu asked the young man, "What is your name?" The young man replied, "My surname is Zhao, and my name is Yanzi." Guan Lu said, "There was nothing else going on earlier; I just felt sorry for you, a young man. You will suddenly die at noon tomorrow, that is why I sighed." Yanzi asked, "Are you not Master Guan Lu?" Guan Lu replied, "I am." Yanzi bowed deeply and followed him, begging for mercy. Guan Lu said, "Fate lies in heaven; it is not within my power to save you."

"Go ahead," [Guan Lu] said, "you should quickly inform your parents, and do not let them be anxious." Yanzi then returned home and hurriedly told his parents. Upon hearing this, his parents immediately mounted their horses and hurried along, covering ten miles before they reached him. They then bowed to Guan Lu and asked him, "My child will die at noon tomorrow. How can you show compassion and save him?" Guan Lu replied, "Just return home and prepare a jar of deer meat and a bottle of clear wine. Arrive at your home on time tomorrow at noon, and only then can he be saved. I do not know if it will work." His father then returned home and prepared the wine and deer meat. The next day, Guan Lu arrived as scheduled and said to Yanzi, "Yesterday, at the large mulberry tree at the south end of the wheat field, two people were playing a dice game. Now, take the wine and deer meat to that place, pour the wine, and place the deer meat there. They will take it themselves."

If I may ask you, if they become angry, [just bow to them, be cautious not to speak, one of them will save you], I am waiting for your news here. "Yanzi followed Guan Lu's advice, and went to the large mulberry tree with wine and meat, where he saw two people playing a game, about to end, with extraordinary attendants. Yanzi then poured wine for them, and the person who received the wine drank it immediately, too engrossed in the game to notice. The wine was almost finished, and the game was about to end, when the person sitting on the north side looked up and saw Yanzi, suddenly becoming very angry and said, "Fool, I sent you to leave early, why did you violate the agreed time? If you don't leave by noon, how could you still pour me wine?" Yanzi bowed twice and dared not speak further. The person sitting on the south side said to the person on the north side, "For every meal a person eats, they owe a favor; for every two meals a person eats, they must help someone."

This morning, he drank his wine and meat. How could we possibly take his life? The person sitting on the north side said, "The case has been decided, how can it be changed?" The person sitting on the south side said, "May I borrow the documents to look at them." He is only nineteen this year, it is easy to change it. He reversed the sentence with his pen and said to Yanzi, "You were destined to die at nineteen, but now you will live to ninety." Since then, there have been instances where documents were reversed, and they were corrected. After returning home, he saw Guan Lu and told Yanzi, "The person sitting on the north side is the Big Dipper, and the person sitting on the south side is the Southern Dipper. All souls pass through the Southern Dipper at the time of conception, and they are filled with joy. The Big Dipper records deaths, and when someone dies, they are filled with sorrow, which is what this is about." (This story is from "Yi Wu Zhi.")

In the past, Duke Jing of Qi had a dream at night where he saw two ill spirits in the form of insects. He sent someone to another country to invite the renowned physician Qin Yuan (or Huan) to the state of Qi. In his dream, the king saw the spirits transform into two young boys dressed in green, standing before him in bed. They spoke to each other, saying, "Qin Yuan is a great and skilled physician. If he comes into the state of Qi, he will surely kill us both. We must devise a plan to escape." One of the boys refused, saying, "Heaven has sent us to take Duke Jing. How can we flee? You are above the area of the heart and lungs, and I am below it. Acupuncture and medicine cannot reach this place. It is a forbidden area. Even if Qin Yuan comes, what can he do to us!" The two boys then transformed back into insects and entered the king's body through his mouth. When he woke up from the dream, he knew he was doomed. Within a few days, Qin Yuan arrived and examined the king's pulse. After a long time, he said to the king, "The illness is incurable." "Why?" the king asked. "Because the illness is in the area above and below the heart and lungs. This is a forbidden area where acupuncture and medicine cannot reach. You will surely die, and there is nothing that can be done." The king replied, "Just as I dreamed." He then did not seek further treatment. Later, he bestowed additional gifts and sent Qin Yuan away with proper ceremony. Three days after Qin Yuan left, the king died. This story is recorded in the *Records of the Grand Historian*.

Once upon a time, there was a man named Liu An from Hejian. When he was young, he fell ill and died, but after seven days, he revived. The emperor allowed him to return home, and he then became skilled in divination. When he divined for others, he still (or especially) knew future events, never missing once. In Hejian, there was a family named Zhao Guang. On their stable, a white horse suddenly transformed into a human face, causing great fear in the household. They went to ask the master diviner Liu An. Liu An said, "This is a great evil omen. You must return home quickly, go three miles away from your house, and cry with your hair loose." All the family members heard the crying and were greatly terrified, so they all rushed out to see what was happening. After the whole family came out, the four-sided tiled house suddenly collapsed. Those who did not come out, the whole family died. Zhao Guang later asked Liu An again, saying, "What kind of disaster is this?" Liu An replied, "There is nothing to it." There are three stone dragons buried three feet deep under the western wall of the main hall. Today, the disaster has passed, but be careful not to disturb them; if you do, it will surely make you poor. If you do not disturb them, you will later achieve wealth and honor; these are divine dragons. Zhao Guang ignored Liu An's advice and went to investigate. He saw a red object as large as a roof beam, which shot up into the sky. After that, Zhao Guang's family became extremely poor, and he lived by begging for food every day. The story is recorded in *The Geography Records*.

Once upon a time, there was a man named Xin Daodu from Longxi. He traveled to study outside his hometown and arrived five miles west of Yongzhou city. He saw a four-sided tile-roofed house with red walls and white pillars, and a young woman in green clothes walking outside the door. Xin Daodu had run out of food and was extremely hungry and thirsty, so he went to the door to beg for food. He told the young woman, "I am Xin Daodu from Longxi, traveling to study elsewhere. I have run out of food. I hope you can tell the master for me to ask for a meal." The girl then went inside to tell the young woman and relayed what Xin Daodu said, informing her. The young woman said, "This person, coming from afar to study, must be a talented person. Tell the guest to come in, I must see him." The girl came out to greet him, but Xin Daodu hurriedly entered and had already reached the entrance. He felt it was not a place of the living and wanted to leave but hesitated, so he didn't dare to return. He then went in to see the Qin maiden. The young woman bowed to each other and finished the greeting. Xin Daodu then asked her to sit on the western bed, and the young woman sat on the eastern bed. They were then served food and drink. The young woman then asked Xin Daodu, "I am the daughter of King Wen of Qin. I suffered a misfortune and without a husband, I live alone. For twenty-three years now, I am in this tomb. Now I have met you. I hope we can become husband and wife. What do you think?" Xin Daodu then asked several questions and followed the rituals of becoming husband and wife. After staying for three days, the young woman told Xin Daodu, "You are a living person, and I am a ghost. Our paths of life and death are different. You should return early, I cannot stay long."

Du asked, "We spent another night weaving together, so what distinguishes today? What can we rely on as a token of trust?" The young woman then took the embroidered pillow from the nine-son deer chest, which was worth a thousand gold pieces, and gave it to Du as a token of trust. There was also a golden pillow in the chest, [Du, being a living person, was greedy for the golden pillow and refused to take the embroidered pillow, wanting the golden pillow instead]. The young woman said, "The golden pillow is a gift from my mother, and I cannot bear to give it to you." Du repeatedly begged for the golden pillow, and the young woman, unable to refuse, gave him the golden pillow as a token of trust. She then sent two maids dressed in blue to escort Du out, and suddenly, the humble home disappeared, replaced by a grand tomb with towering pine and cypress trees. Du was terrified and rushed through the forest to escape the tomb. When he looked back, the golden pillow was still in his arms, and he decided to go to the market in Qin to sell it.

At that time, King Wen of Qin's wife happened to be riding in a carriage to the market to watch, and she saw a golden pillow. Recognizing it, she asked Du, "Where did you get this?" Du told the truth in reply. The lady then wept bitterly, sobbing so much that she couldn't bear it, and sent someone to inform King Qin. The king said he didn't believe, and sent soldiers to open the tomb and the coffin to look inside. All the burial items were present, only the golden pillow was missing. Unbound and looked at it, there was a place where husband and wife performed their rites. King Qin and his wife then became happy, saying with a sigh, "My daughter has such great virtue that she communicates with the divine, she was able to marry a living person, truly my daughter's husband." Then made Du a Duke of the Imperial Bride, rewarded him with jade, silk, carriages, horses, and attendants, and ordered him to return to his hometown. From then on, later people followed this example, the husband of the king's daughter was called 'Duke of the Imperial Bride,' passing down through generations without end. The story is recorded in the *Records of the Grand Historian*.

At that time, Lady of King Wen of Qin was seen entering the marketplace in her carriage to watch the scene. She then saw the golden pillow and recognized it. She asked Du, "Where did you get this?" Du truthfully answered, and upon hearing this, the lady wept bitterly, choked with sobs, and could not bear the grief herself. She then sent envoys to inform King of Qin. The king, however, did not believe her, so he sent soldiers to open the tomb and examine its contents. All the items used in the funeral were present, except for the golden pillow. They then unbound the body and examined it, and found the place where the couple had performed the wedding rites. Upon this discovery, King of Qin and his wife were overjoyed and exclaimed, "My daughter possesses a saintly virtue that connects with the divine, enabling her to marry a living person—truly, he is my son-in-law." They then conferred upon Du the title of *Imperial Son-in-Law Commissioner*, bestowing upon him gifts of jade, silk, chariots, horses, and attendants, and ordered him to return to his hometown. From that time onward, later generations followed this example, and the husband of a royal princess came to be known as "phu ma" (*驸马*), a title that has been passed down through the ages without interruption. This story is recorded in the *Shiji* (*Records of the Grand Historian*).

A Man Named Hou Hua There was once a man named Hou Hua from Baima County. While working in the fields, he heard a crying sound but could not see its form, and this continued for more than sixty days. During the autumn, while walking through the fields, the dew was so wet that it was difficult to enter. He then pulled up his clothes and entered the ground from the edge. Near the edge, he found a dead person's skull, half above ground and half below. In the eye socket, a stalk of rice had grown, and it was already about to sprout. Hou Hua felt compassion for it and pulled it out. The skull and the earth covered it, forming a small mound. From then on, the crying sound ceased. Later, in August, Hou Hua was cutting rice in the fields until evening, when he returned home. He felt someone following him from behind. If he hurried, the person also hurried; if he walked slowly, the person also walked slowly. Hou Hua found this strange and asked, "Who are you, and why are you following me?" The answer was, "I am a ghost." Hou Hua said, "I am a living person, and you are a dead ghost. We have different paths, so why are you following me?" The ghost replied, "I was grateful for your kindness when you pulled out the rice. I have nothing to repay you with. Knowing you do not yet have a wife, I will arrange for you to get married on the first day of the eleventh month next year. You should treat her with the respect due to a living person." Hou Hua accepted this and kept silent. Then, on the first day of the eleventh month, he gathered relatives and family, slaughtered a cow, and brewed wine, all in preparation for the wedding, not knowing where the bride would come from.

Huo's parents, brothers, and relatives found him strange, and when they asked, he did not explain the reason. He often stood in the south of the village, waiting incessantly. When it neared the time of the afternoon meal, yellow dust, clouds, and sudden rain came from the west, until it reached the door of Huo's house. The clouds and fog were dark, and one could not see each other. Huo then entered the room, where there was a girl, about eighteen or nineteen years old, with her bed, bedding, and personal belongings, which were beyond description. Seeing Huo enter, the girl spoke to him, saying, "Who are you, entering my room?" Huo replied to the girl, "Madam, who are you, entering my room?" The girl then said to Huo, "I am the daughter of Governor Liang Helong of Liaoxi, who is now married to the son of Governor Mao Boda of Liaodong. Today, the carriage for my wedding is at the door. Because of the strong wind, I came out to look at it and then returned home to my room. This is not your room!" Huo said, "Liaoxi is more than five thousand miles away. Why, madam, are you disputing this room with me? If you do not believe me, please go out and see for yourself." The girl, startled, went out and saw that it was not her own house. She then took a small chest from behind the bed, opened it, and found a jade tablet with clear golden characters, which said, "Heaven has destined me to be the wife of Hou Huo." Since then, later people have learned from this, creating marriage tablets and wedding documents, thus giving rise to this custom. Even the dead can repay gratitude, how much more so the living. The matter is recorded in the *Shiji*.

In ancient times, there were two brothers named Hou Guang and Hou Zhou, who were born in the same household and often traveled together, carrying much wealth to distant places for trade. Hou Guang's trade was highly profitable, which made Hou Zhou envious. He harbored evil intentions and killed his brother at the edge of Guo Huan's land, throwing the body into the jungle. Then, he returned home first. When Hou Guang's parents asked Hou Zhou, "You came early, where is your brother?" Hou Zhou replied, "My brother will come in twenty years." Guo Huan was working in the fields near this area when he heard birds crying chaotically in the jungle. He found it strange and went to investigate, where he discovered a dead body. Feeling compassion, he returned home, took a shovel, and buried the body. After finishing his work, his family would send food every day during the middle of the month to worship the body. After more than ninety days, when the wheat and barley were harvested, Guo Huan wanted to return home. He bid farewell to the dead body, making a vow, saying, "I buried your corpse here. I will worship you every day for three months. I don't know your name, so from now on, I will not worship you. You must fend for yourself." They then parted ways.

The following April, Guo Huan was working in the fields when suddenly a person appeared before him. The person asked, "Who are you, and why are you standing in front of me?" Guo Huan replied, "I am a living person, and you are a ghost. We come from different worlds and places, so how did you come here?" The ghost answered, "I was deeply grateful to you for your kindness in the past, but I had nothing to repay you. Today, my family has a great feast, so I invited you, and I also have gifts to give you. I will not break this promise." Guo Huan was suspicious, but he followed the ghost. The ghost was covered by a supernatural presence, making Guo Huan invisible to others. In a moment, the ghost led Guo Huan to a spirit bed. On the offering tray, there was plenty of food. Both Hou Guang and Guo Huan ate until they finished. The relatives were amazed and called it a miraculous event. Shortly after, Hou Zhou, the younger brother, came to check on his older brother. When Hou Guang suddenly saw his younger brother, he told Guo Huan, "This is the person who killed me. He killed me when I was alive, and even in death, I am afraid of him." Hou Guang was so frightened that he ran out. Guo Huan, without the supernatural cover, revealed himself and stood up from the spirit bed. He explained everything to Hou Guang's parents and brothers, and they took Hou Zhou to the county office. When questioned, Hou Zhou confessed to the crime. Hou Guang's parents rewarded Guo Huan with money, vehicles, horses, and attendants, and sent him to retrieve the ghost of their son to bury him. Thus, it is said: Hou Guang, even as a ghost, still repaid a debt of gratitude, let alone a living person. This story is recorded in the *Shiji* (Records of the Grand Historian).

Once upon a time, there was a man named Wang Jingbo from Kuaiji. He was traveling by boat to the Liao River to trade. At the same time, Governor Liu Huiming of Kuaiji had completed his term of office and was returning home with the spirit of his deceased daughter. They were together with Wang Jingbo. The governor was deeply worried and pensive. On a bright moonlit, quiet night, he took out his lute and played it. The sound was mournful and poignant. Hearing the mournful and plaintive sound of the lute, the spirit of the dead girl rose to listen and began to play with her hairpins and bracelets. It sounded like someone was speaking. "Why don't you come into the boat?" the governor asked. "Just come in, what do you have to doubt?" the spirit replied. She came forward and entered, accompanied by two maids. Her appearance was beautiful and dignified, and she was so lifelike that it was almost confusing. He immediately invited her to sit down, and after she had warmed up and settled in, the governor asked, "Why did you come alone at night to this place?" The spirit replied, "I also know a little about playing the lute." The two maids brought her blankets and quilts, and brought wine, meat, and food. They had a banquet together. After the banquet was over, the governor returned to playing the lute, producing several melodies, and then gave it to the spirit. The spirit of the dead girl took the lute and praised the mournful sound as very beautiful. Late at night, almost dawn, they could be intimate. After the spirit finished singing and returned the lute, the governor played with her and composed a poem saying: "Tonight I lament my loneliness and sorrow, the grief and resentment are endless. Oh, you have such virtue, we can be intimate together tonight."

The young lady said, "If you truly grieve over my favor, I will report it separately." She stopped playing the zither and extinguished the candle, sending the maid out of the boat. The two of them drank thoroughly, no different from living people. By the fourth watch of the night, the young lady got up to comb her hair, weeping sadly, and also did not speak. Mr. Jingbo asked, "Who is the young lady? What is her surname and given name? When will we meet again?" The young lady replied, "I am now in the underworld, unseen for seven years. I heard you playing the sad zither alone, so I came to explain. Now I must leave, and we may not meet again." They held hands and parted, and she suddenly disappeared. Mr. Jingbo's tears welled up in his eyes, and he spoke with great emotion, thinking of her beautiful face, choked with sorrow. After a long sigh, he immediately went into the boat and sat down. It was gradually becoming light, and the clothes and other items near the body of Lady Hui were missing. After searching, they were found on Mr. Jingbo's boat, and he immediately wanted to report to the authorities. Mr. Jingbo said, "Last night, in the lonely and quiet night, under the moonlight, I was playing the zither when suddenly a young lady and two maids came into my boat, played the zither and had fun, and left at the fourth watch. She gave me a traveling tent, a pair of threads, and a brocade quilt, as a token. I gave her a toothpick, a white bone cage, a pair of gold bracelets, and a pair of silver rings. I hope you will check the items near the body of the young lady. If they are not there, you can report to the authorities." Lady Hui, upon hearing the story of the couple, followed the good and bad omens, and those who heard it all said, "How strange!"

In the past, during the Qin dynasty, the governor of Hanling, Zhao Ziyuan, went out of the city and saw a young woman with beautiful features, who was about fifteen or sixteen years old. He asked, "Where is this girl from, and why is she alone?" The girl replied, "I am a guest staying outside the city, so I have no companion." The governor did not know that she was a ghost and asked her, "Can you make clothes? My family hires someone to make clothes." The girl said, "I am good at making clothes." He then brought her to his home. The governor sent her some colorful fabrics to make clothes and gave her five hundred coins. Over three years, she visited the governor's home regularly, and because the governor pitied her, he always gave her more money. [When she was about to leave, she returned to the governor's home one last time, and he gave her a branch of gold, two pairs of gold hairpins, and two pieces of silk.] The girl bowed twice and said farewell to the governor, saying, "I will return to my hometown tomorrow at noon, and I will not come back." The governor sent someone to see her off at the gate, and she left. [The next day, a hundred and fifty steps south of the city, there was a tomb where the girl had died.] The girl's parents [returned home] to receive her spirit and bury her in the tomb. When they opened the coffin, they found countless gold hairpins, gold bars, and two pieces of silk. Her parents were shocked and puzzled, and they could not understand why this had happened. [The girl had worked for the governor, and he had given her these things.] Her death was so unusual that it was beyond ordinary understanding. [The story is from the *Jinzhuan* (Records of the Jin Dynasty).]

In ancient times, during the reign of Liu Quan (Yuan), Liang Yuanhao and Duan Zijing were both from Pingyang. When they were young, they loved each other deeply, lived opposite each other, and went out and came in together, highly respecting each other. They formed a friendship and swore not to abandon each other. As they grew older, both exhibited the spirit of excellence and talent, serving under Liu Quan. Yuanhao became the Left Chancellor of the Shangshu Province, while Zijing became the Attendant of the Yellow Gate. Although their official positions were different, the two loved each other so much that they did not part from each other day or night. From the emperor down, all knew of it. Later, Liu Quan appointed Yuanhao as the governor of Jingzhou and Zijing as the governor of Qinzhou. The two began to separate, each going to their respective posts. After three years, Yuanhao died in Jingzhou, passing away due to losing his voice. However, before the news was sent, he missed Zijing in his heart and wanted to entrust his afterlife affairs. Now, being unable to speak, he had no way to express himself. He stopped for ten days, and his spirit appeared to him, not allowing burial until Zijing arrived. His wife and children were terrified, not knowing what to do. The spirit of Yuanhao went to Qinzhou and conveyed a dream to Zijing, saying, "Because of illness, I died, and I wanted to see my brother face-to-face, and now I have seen him. I left words for my wife and children, but they did not understand my words. They are about to bury me, but I have not yet said goodbye to my brother, so I am staying at home. You should quickly go and bury me." Zijing woke up from his sleep, suddenly dreaming and sitting in awe, exclaiming, "Why did Yuanhao die? His spirit has always been so different from mine in life. Considering the words in this dream, they must not be false."

Zi Jing suddenly awoke, hastily sending a memorial, riding a postal horse to rush to the capital, where everything matched his dream exactly. He wept aloud, dying and then reviving, almost reaching the afternoon, filled with frustration, resentment, and sighing. Suddenly, he went out to see, and Yuan Hao appeared before him, still unchanged from life. Yuan Hao said, "You buried me, and death is a sweet farewell. In the box at the west end of my bed, there are seven volumes of my writings, one jade nail for the zither, one sandalwood如意杖, as a token for you. I hope you take them. If you miss me, take them and practice them." Zi Jing replied, "I came from the north in a hurry, and I have nothing else. I then took off a pair of boot straps, as a token for you, brother." The two of them were very affectionate, and then they parted. Zi Jing returned inside and told Yuan Hao's wife about this matter. Yuan Hao then tied the strap that Zi Jing had given him into a knot of mutual affection and tied it to his own feet. Everyone saw and said it was strange. After the funeral was over, Zi Jing then returned to Qin Prefecture. After one year, it was said that the magistrate of Tai Shan had died, and Yama King, after sixty days, could not find a suitable person. Hao remembered Zi Jing, so he went before the king and praised Governor Duan Zi Jing of Qin Prefecture for his clear mind and diligence, very practical and upright, capable of being the magistrate. The king can summon and appoint him. The king asked, "How long is his lifespan?" The ghost officials were ordered to check Zi Jing's life account, which showed he was supposed to live to ninety-seven, but now he was only thirty-two. The king said, "Although he is a good person, his lifespan has not run out. Should not die prematurely. Summon him to serve."

Hao addressed Wang again, saying, "Because Zi Jing and I have been close friends since childhood, our relationship is as inseparable as fish and water. If he were not a good person, how dare I recommend him? I will personally call him to come, with my attendants, and Zi Jing will surely come happily." Thus, Wang provided Hao with attendants and a strong escort, and Hao went to Qin Prefecture to call Zi Jing. Hao transformed into a living person, with a dignified procession, riding a horse and traveling. Everyone who saw him avoided the path and passed by, wanting to reach Qin Prefecture, he first sent someone to notify. Zi Jing suddenly became startled, as Yuan Hao had already died, how could he come here? He then ran out to welcome him, and they entered the hall together and sat down. After a while, they served food and wine. The children of the state and Zi Jing's family, seeing the good guest, all came, not knowing that Yuan Hao was a ghost. After the meal, the two went into the room and sat down. Yuan Hao then said, "Wang has sent me to call you, intending to give you the position of Chief Clerk in Tai Shan. You must go now." Zi Jing was unhappy, and suddenly cried and said, "A man like me, a governor of Qin Prefecture or a governor of Fang Prefecture, becomes a low-ranking official in Tai Shan, an official position cannot be lowly or insignificant." Yuan Hao said, "That is not the case. A living official is lowly, but a dead official's position cannot be compared." Yuan Hao was afraid that Zi Jing would not go, so he stood up, drew his sword, and was about to kill him to demonstrate his power and force him to go. Zi Jing knew he could not escape, so he begged for one more year. Yuan Hao said, "Yama has temporarily stopped the selection and is waiting for you. You must go now, and you cannot delay any further."

Zijin said, "If as my elder brother says, how dare I not let my younger brother and his family say goodbye?" Hao replied, "If I say I will obey, then let me stay for three more days, and at noon I will come to you. My younger brother must prepare thoroughly and accompany me." Thus, the two parted ways after seeing each other off. After parting, Zijin immediately called his relatives to say goodbye and ordered the preparation of coffins, bedding, and other burial items, everything was prepared meticulously. Both inner and outer relatives and officials of the prefecture and county were all surprised and immediately asked, "Your Excellency's household is peaceful and safe, why are you preparing such ominous items? What do you intend to use them for?" Zijin said, "I have seen Liang Yuanhao as a friend, and he died first. Now he has reported to the Yama King, who has summoned me, and we are to meet. I must not miss the time."

Duan Zijin bathed in fragrant water, dressed himself, and stepped out to look afar. He saw Liáng Yuánhào's entourage of horsemen and attendants approaching. He then told his wife and family, "I am going to die now. When the magistrate arrives at the door, I cannot stay long. You all should bid me farewell. [After the farewells were completed], take a quilt and cover my face." And so he died immediately. One year after Duan Zijin's death, he returned to his house, stayed there for three months, and then left again. Those who saw him all said it was strange, realizing that Duan Zijin being the chief clerk of Tai Mountain was not false. Therefore, the saying goes: "Liáng Yuánhào's death led to the early death of Duan Zijin." The benefits of good and bad fortune vary widely, and there are countless paths in life. Prince Wáng Zhen gained ghostly power, but Duan Zijin suffered ghostly harm. Hence, it is said: "The forces at work are different," and this is precisely the case. [This story is from the "Aoyan Zhuan" (Tales of the Strange)]. In ancient times, there was a man named Duan Xiaozhen, from Jingzhao. During the reign of Emperor Jing of Han, he was appointed as the county magistrate of Chang'an. Duan Xiaozhen was known for his clear and diligent nature, and his reputation spread far and wide. He had a horse that was very fast, capable of traveling more than five hundred miles in a day. The governor of Yongzhou, Liáng Yuánwéi, relied on his connections and influence. Seeing that Duan Xiaozhen's horse was excellent, he asked for it. Duan Xiaozhen replied, "This horse is already old and not suitable for riding. It is also the horse my father used, and I am reluctant to part with it. I dare not give it to you, sir." Liáng Yuánwéi was displeased and privately sent someone to accuse Duan Xiaozhen of taking things, leading to his imprisonment without allowing his family to visit.

Zhen knew he was being wrongfully killed and secretly sent someone to privately inform his son: "The governor is preparing to kill me with this horse. Regretting that you are still young and have not obtained official positions, you should buy 300 sheets of fine paper, five pens, and ten sticks of ink. When I am buried, place them in front of me, and I will present my case." The governor ordered Zhen to be beaten to death in prison. After more than a month, Emperor Jing of Han convened a grand assembly of ministers during the political session. Zhen immediately presented a memorial to the palace, detailing the crimes of Liang Yuanwei. Zhen then appeared as a living person, explaining that Liang Yuanwei was corrupt and unjustly killed him. He listed the crimes of Liang Yuanwei as follows: "I humbly request Your Majesty to investigate on my behalf." Emperor Jing received the memorial and suddenly Zhen disappeared. Surprised, Emperor Jing said, "Within the universe, such a thing has never been seen." He then arrested Liang Yuanwei and investigated according to the charges, and the matter was true. The emperor realized that Zhen had been wrongfully killed and executed Liang Yuanwei and other criminals at Zhen's tomb. He then appointed Zhen's son as the magistrate of Chang'an. Do not say that ghosts have no supernatural power; it is the divine power of Duan Zhen that moved this. This is recorded in the *Book of Curious Tales*.

A Tale of Fated Love in the Era of Qin Shi Huang Once upon a time during the reign of Qin Shi Huang, there was a man named Wang Dao Peng from the county of Jiu. When he was young, he and the daughter of a village man named Tang Shu Xie, whose name was Wen Yu Hua, became acquainted through their shared beauty and eventually married. Wang Dao Peng was later conscripted into military service and ended up in the southern frontier, where he did not return for nine years. Wen Yu's parents, seeing that Wang Dao Peng had not returned, wanted to betroth their daughter to Liu Yuanxiang. However, the daughter had already formed a deep bond with Wang Dao Peng and refused to remarry. Despite their efforts, her parents pressured her, and she eventually married Liu Yuanxiang. After three years, the daughter died of anger. Three years after her death, Wang Dao Peng returned home. He asked the villagers if the daughter was still alive. They replied, "The daughter had married Liu Yuanxiang and had died three years ago." Wang Dao Peng then found out where her grave was, went to the tomb, called out her name three times, and wept bitterly. After a long while, she revived. She walked around the grave three times and then spoke, saying, "We had always intended to stay together, whether life or death. I was entangled in official duties, which led to our separation for so long."

With the same hope as before, I will go to see you briefly. If there are divine beings, let me see you; if there are no divine beings, then we will part forever. The young lady then appeared before me, just as she was when she was alive, and inquired about my well-being: "Our original emotional bond is extremely important, but due to the pressure from my parents, you will never come back to me, so I married into the Liu family and have been married for three years. I miss you day and night deeply and died out of resentment. Now that I have returned, we will become husband and wife again. Quickly dig up the grave and break open the coffin, and I will definitely come back to life." Wang Ping said, "If what you say is true, it is indeed a spiritual connection, rare in heaven and earth. If one believes, it is the foundation of one's character." Wang Ping then quickly dug up the grave and broke open the coffin. The young lady immediately got up and dressed, and followed Wang Ping back home. Later, her husband Liu Yuanxiang was surprised and puzzled, deeply regretting and finding it strange. He went through the state to report, speaking of Wang Ping. The state and county had no legal basis to decide, so they reported to Emperor Qin Shi Huang. Emperor Qin Shi Huang decided that Wang Dao would marry Wang Ping, and she lived for a hundred years before dying.

Once upon a time, there was a man named Liu Ji from Fengyi County. He took a cow and went to Yingshou Market to sell it, obtaining twenty-three bolts of silk. He returned home. After traveling one hundred and ninety li to the city, he lodged at the inn of Master Wang Seng for the night. Wang Seng, along with his two brothers, killed Liu Ji, threw his body into a dry well in the eastern garden, and buried it there. However, Liu Ji's spirit communicated with his family. That very night, he dreamed to his brother, saying, "[Yesterday, when going to] Yingshou to sell the cow, I obtained twenty-three bolts of silk. On my return journey, I traveled one hundred and ninety li south of the city, [lodge at Master Wang Seng's inn, where the master killed me, buried me in the well in the eastern garden of his house, and hid the silk in a cabinet in the south-facing room of the eastward house]." His brother, upon waking from the dream, was filled with fear. Now, with this matter, he felt grief, resentment, and longing. His younger brother had been killed by a bandit, and he had dreamed the message, which must surely be true. He immediately visited Wang Seng's house, found his younger brother's body in the dry well in the eastern garden, and discovered the original twenty-three bolts of silk in a cabinet in the south-facing room of the eastward house, just as the divine dream had foretold. They immediately captured Wang Seng and sent him to the state for investigation. Everything was found to be true. In the marketplace, people thought and sought the words of the ghost, greatly relying on them. Thus, they communicated with the gods, and the matter was reported immediately. The story is recorded in *The Southern Strange Tales* (《南妖皇（异）记》).

There was once King Xuan of Zhou, who believed slanderous words and unjustly killed the loyal minister Du Bo. At the moment of his death, Du Bo looked up to the sky and said, "I am innocent, but the king bent to take slanderous and flattery words and unjustly killed me. Now I am dead, knowing my innocence, what more can I say? If I am to precede the world, after three years, I will surely kill the king, and the king will not know." The king heard this and became very angry, saying, "I am the ruler of ten thousand chariots; even if I unjustly kill three or five people, what sin is there!" He then killed Du Bo. After another three years, King Xuan went out of the city to hunt, traveling to the south gate of the city, where he saw Du Bo with his attendants and ghost soldiers in formation, riding a red horse and wearing a red cap, magnificent and holding a bow and arrow, aiming at King Xuan and shooting him. The king fled but had no way out, and all the officials and below saw it. The arrow hit the king's heart, and the king's heart was in pain, then immediately returned to the palace. Within three days, King Xuan died. An ancient poem says, "One should not unjustly kill people, for retribution will soon come." The matter is recorded in the *Taishi*.

Once upon a time, there was a man named Liu Yidi from Zhongshan, who was exceptionally skilled at making a legendary wine called the thousand-day wine. Anyone who drank it would fall into a deep sleep for a thousand days. At that time, Liu Xuan Shi from Qingzhou was known for his extraordinary ability to drink vast amounts of alcohol. He came to Liu Yidi to try the thousand-day wine. Liu Yidi told Liu Xuan Shi, "The wine is still boiling and not ready, it cannot be drunk by you." Liu Xuan Shi repeatedly begged to taste it. Liu Yidi then took a cup and gave it to him. After finishing the cup, Liu Xuan Shi asked for more. Liu Yidi knew he would be drunk, so he said to Liu Xuan Shi, "You are now drunk, wait until you wake up, and then come back to drink together." Liu Xuan Shi became angry and left. When Liu Xuan Shi returned home, he died from the intoxication. His family did not know the cause and buried him. Three years later, Liu Yidi went to visit Liu Xuan Shi's house and asked about him. The family was surprised and said, "Liu Xuan Shi has been dead for three years, and we have completed our mourning period. We are just now looking for him." Liu Yidi explained, "When we were drinking together, you should have just woken up. Just go to dig up the grave and break the coffin, and you will see that he is not dead." The family followed his instructions and opened the grave. They saw Liu Xuan Shi with white sweat on his face, opening his eyes and lying down. He then got up and said, "Who are you people? Why are you in front of me? I was drunk and slept, and now I have just woken up." The people who saw him still smelled the wine on him and were still drunk for three days. Those who saw him all said it was amazing.

During the reign of Wu King Sun Quan, there was a man named Li Chun from Jina, a district of Xiangyang. He had a dog named Wulong, whom he loved very much. Wherever Li Chun went or sat, the dog always accompanied him. One day, after Li Chun's wife's family had a drinking party, Li Chun fell asleep in the grass in a field along the road. At that time, the governor of Xiangyang, Liu Xia, was out hunting and saw that the grass in the area was very deep. Not knowing that Li Chun was lying in the grass and asleep, he sent people to set fire to it. When the dog saw the fire approaching, it tried to pull Li Chun away with its mouth, but it could not succeed. The dog then, about sixty steps north of where Li Chun was lying, found a water ditch. It jumped into the water, twisting and turning to wet its body, and came back to the four sides of where Li Chun was lying, making the grass around him wet. When the fire reached the wet grass, it immediately went out. Li Chun was saved from danger, but the dog burned to death. The governor and the villagers made a coffin and a grave, over a thousand feet high, and buried it with proper rites. Today, in Jina, there is a grave for the loyal dog, which is this one. Those who heard about it all said: "How extraordinary! Even a dog can repay its master's kindness. How much more so a human!"

Once upon a time, there was a man named Li Xin from Xinyi in Chenliu. He was known for his kindness and filial piety, always taking good care of his parents. At the age of thirty-eight, he had a dream in which a messenger of death came to take him away. They brought him before Yama, the king of hell, who then judged him according to the law. Li Xin appealed to Yama, saying, "I and my elderly mother both suffer greatly. I have lost the protection of my father, and now that my life is over, I dare not disobey. But my mother is old and alone, and after I leave, there will be no one to take care of her. I humbly beg Your Majesty's mercy, asking for my life to be spared so that I can continue to care for her." Yama asked how many years were left in Li Xin's mother's life, and the messenger replied, "According to the records, she has a lifespan of ninety years, and she still has twenty-seven years remaining." Yama said, "Since she has twenty-seven years left, I will show compassion and let him go." The messenger then added, "There are many people like Li Xin in the world. If we spare him, others might follow suit." Hearing this, Yama reconsidered and decided to let Li Xin die. The spirits were angry with Li Xin for his appeal and cut off his head and hands, throwing him into a pot of boiling water. At that moment, Yama sent someone to call him back, intending to let him return home to care for his mother. The messenger said, "Your head and hands have already been damaged in the boiling water, and you cannot get them back. We will give you substitute head and hands, and once you pass by Yama, you can come back here with your good head and hands. Be careful not to leave prematurely."

Due to urgent circumstances, it was decided to give you a barbarian head, but the king would release you to return home to care for your old mother. Xin heard he was being released and felt joy, so he immediately returned, forgetting to let the ghost messenger take a good head and hands. Suddenly, he woke up from a dream, and his head and hands were both of the barbarian. Xin became distressed and told his wife, "Do you recognize my voice?" His wife replied, "My voice is common, what is so unusual?" Xin said, "I dreamed of something strange last night. If you wake up in the morning, cover my head and face with a blanket. If you want to bring food to the bed, close the door and go away, get the food yourself." His wife followed his instructions, took the blanket, covered him, and left. When she brought the food, she told her husband, "What strange thing happened?" Suddenly, she made a sound and looked under the blanket, and there was a barbarian on a bed. His wife was shocked and frightened, ran to tell her mother-in-law, "My son did something strange last night. Now there is a Brahmin barbarian on the new bride's bed." The mother-in-law heard this and beat Xin's head and face with a stick, not listening to his explanations. Neighbors who heard the noise came, asked about the cause of the incident, and Xin finally explained the whole story. They finally realized it was their son and hugged and cried. The Han emperor heard about it and asked curiously, "From ancient times to the present, I have never heard of such a thing. Even though it was a false claim of a barbarian head, it shows the utmost filial piety, which connects with the divine." He immediately appointed Xin as the Doctor of Filial Piety and Righteousness. The power of the divine dream is so great, how extraordinary!

In ancient times, there was a man named Wang Zizhen from Taiyuan. His parents loved him dearly and lamented, saying, "Our son has not yet had the opportunity to pursue good learning." They then sent him to study under the scholar Bian Xiao in Dingzhou. Master Bian was from Xinyi in Chenliu, and he was widely knowledgeable in ancient texts and history, able to answer any question without limit. After the death of Confucius, there was no one else like Master Bian; he had three thousand disciples, none of whom could resist his teachings. No one in the world could surpass him, and thus people from all corners of the earth came to learn from Master Bian. Wang Zizhen traveled thirty miles into Dingzhou's territory and stopped to rest under a willow tree by the roadside. There, a ghost transformed into a living person and also rested under the same tree. Wang Zizhen believed the figure to be a real person and did not realize it was a ghost.

Zhen then asked, "Where are you from?" The ghost replied to Zhen, "Where are you from?" Zhen answered, "My parents sent me to study with Mr. Bian in Dingzhou because of my shallow knowledge, and there are no other matters." The ghost asked Zhen again, "What is your surname and given name?" Zhen said, "My surname is Wang, and my given name is Zizhen. I am from Taiyuan." The ghost said, "I am from Bohai, my surname is Li, and my given name is Xuan. My parents died early, and my brothers and I live together. My elder brother sent me to study with Mr. Bian because I have not been educated. From now on, we will study together." Seeing that the ghost was older, Zhen got up and bowed to Li Xuan, acknowledging him as a brother. They traveled together to the host's house in Dingzhou, drank wine, and made a pact to be friends. They vowed that their friendship would not be broken by life or death, wealth or poverty. During his three years of study, Li Xuan surpassed Mr. Bian in talent and skill. Mr. Bian asked Li Xuan, "Are you not a sage? Why are you so different from others? You were already very talented before, but now you are even better than Li Xuan. What other skills do you have? I wish you would teach me one." Li Xuan then bowed to Mr. Bian again and said, "I have been fortunate to have the opportunity to study under your guidance, but I do not know why things have turned out this way."

Mr. Bian immediately appointed Xuan as an assistant professor to teach the disciples. All were intimidated by Xuan, and as a result, the school became a place where there were no illegal acts. If anyone committed an illegal act, they would be punished. He still taught Zizhen privately to understand the meaning. If he did not understand, he would be punished. Zizhen treated Xuan like a master and did not act independently. Zizhen's learning was thus completed. Later, there was Wang Zhongxiang, a prince's attendant from Taiyuan, who was slightly related to Zizhen. He came to visit the school and stayed overnight together. He realized that Li Xuan was a ghost. The next day on the road, he held hands with Zizhen to say goodbye. He then told Zizhen, "I am related to you, and now I have seen something strange, so I cannot not tell you. Your friends now are not good people." Zizhen replied, "Li Xuan today, if we talk about learning, he is a scholar and gentleman, as for appearance, he is rare in the world, what else could be complained about, saying they are not good people?" Wang Zhongxiang said, "What I am talking about is not about people's appearance or character."

Mr. Bian appointed Li Xuan as an assistant professor, who then taught the disciples. All the students revered Li Xuan, and as a result, the school became orderly, with none of them committing any improper acts. Should anyone commit an offense, they would be punished accordingly. Li Xuan also continued to teach Zi Zhen privately in a secluded room, explaining the meaning of the classics. If Zi Zhen failed to understand, he would be punished. Zi Zhen treated Li Xuan with the reverence of a master, no longer acting on his own accord. Thus, Zi Zhen’s learning was completed. Later, there came Wang Zhongxiang, an attendant to the crown prince, who was from Taiyuan. He had previously had a slight acquaintance with Zi Zhen and so came to visit the school. One night, they stayed together, and then he realized that Li Xuan was a ghost. The next day, on the road, he clasped Zi Zhen’s hand to say goodbye and then said to him, "I am related to you by kinship, and now I have witnessed something strange, which I cannot remain silent about. Your current friend is not a good person." Zi Zhen replied, "If we consider Li Xuan’s learning today, he is a scholar-gentleman, and as for his appearance, it is rare in the world. What more is there to complain about? Why say he is not a good person?" [Wang Zhongxiang said], "What I am saying is not about human appearance or conduct."

The Younger Brother and the Ghost I am a living person, and Li Xuan is a ghost; life and death are distinct, so how can we be friends? If you do not believe me, tonight take a bundle of fresh grass, spread it out and lie down on it, while I lie down separately. In the morning, check the grass; where I lay, the grass will be pressed down, and where the ghost lay, the grass will be loose. Afterward, check the grass in the morning, and as Wang Zhongxiang predicted, I realized that Li Xuan was indeed a ghost. I politely said to Li Xuan, "There are rumors outside saying that you are a ghost, is this true?" Li Xuan replied, "I am a ghost. Last night, Wang Zhongxiang came and realized that I was a ghost, so he told you. Who knows my transformation? Only Yama, the king of hell, saw that I was young and appointed me to a position of responsibility. He sent me to learn from Bian Xiansheng because my knowledge was limited. If I achieve a certain level of knowledge within three years, I will be appointed as the magistrate of Tai Shan. If I do not reach that level, I will return to being a common person. Under Bian Xiansheng's guidance, I achieved my knowledge in less than a year and have been serving as the magistrate of Tai Shan for two years. I stayed because you had not returned home, and I was attached to the family."
